critique.summary: The article introduces Manyana, a project by Bram Cohen that demonstrates a new approach to version control using CRDTs (Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types). It highlights how Manyana improves conflict resolution and history management compared to traditional version control systems.
mainPoints: Manyana uses CRDTs to ensure merges never fail and always produce a consistent result regardless of merge order.
Conflict presentation in Manyana is more informative, showing what changes were made and by whom, rather than opaque conflict markers.
The system maintains a 'weave' of all lines ever added or removed, eliminating the need for common ancestor traversal in merges.
Rebasing in Manyana can preserve full history without creating fictional commit sequences, unlike traditional rebasing.
Manyana is currently a demo implemented in about 470 lines of Python, focusing on single-file operations without full version control features yet.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about CRDTs and their properties are consistent with established computer science literature on CRDTs, which guarantee eventual consistency and conflict-free merges. Bram Cohen is a credible figure in software development, known for BitTorrent, lending credibility to the technical insights. The description of Manyana as a demo project aligns with the available public information. However, the article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, limiting verification of practical usability or adoption.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential performance implications or scalability of CRDT-based version control in large projects.
There is no comparison with other emerging version control approaches or existing tools that attempt to improve merge conflicts.
User experience challenges beyond conflict presentation, such as integration with existing workflows or tooling, are not addressed.
The article does not provide independent evaluations or user feedback on Manyana's effectiveness.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the potential and limitations of using CRDTs for version control systems, with many commenters expressing skepticism about CRDTs fully solving semantic merge conflicts. Several highlight existing tools and approaches that address usability and merge conflict presentation, while others emphasize the need for improvements in scalability, semantic understanding, and team workflows.

comments.author: theknarf
comments.text: You can't use CRDTs for version control, having conflicts is the whole point of version control. Sometimes two developers will make changes that fundamentally tries to change the code in two different ways, a merge conflict then leaves it up to the developer who is merging/rebasing to make a choice about the semantics of the program they want to keep. A CRDT would just produce garbage code, its fundamentally the wrong solution. If you want better developer UX for merge conflicts then there are both a bunch of tooling on top of Git, as well as other version control systems, that try to present it in a better way; but that has very little to do with the underlaying datastructure. The very fact that cherry-picking and reverting becomes difficult with this approach should show you that its the wrong approach! Those are really easy operations to do in Git.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately points out that CRDTs cannot resolve semantic conflicts inherent in version control, emphasizing that merge conflicts require human judgment, which CRDTs alone do not provide.

comments.author: aggregator-ios
comments.text:
What CRDT's solve is conflicts at the system level. Not at the semantic level. 2 or more engineers setting a var to a different value cannot be handled by a CRDT.

Engineer A intended value = 1

Engineer B intended value = 2

CRDT picks 2

The outcome could be semantically wrong. It doesn't reflect the intent.

I think the primary issue with git and every other version control is the terrible names for everything. pull, push, merge, fast forward, stash, squash, rebase, theirs, ours, origin, upstream and that's just a subset. And the GUI's. They're all very confusing even to engineers who have been doing this for a decade. On top of this, conflict resolution is confusing because you don't have any prior warnings.

It would be incredibly useful if before you were about to edit a file, the version control system would warn you that someone else has made changes to it already or are actively working on it. In large teams, this sort of automation would reduce conflicts, as long as humans agree to not touch the same file. This would also reduce the amount of quality regressions that result from bad conflict resolutions.

Shameless self plug: I am trying to solve both issues with a simpler UI around git that automates some of this and it's free. https://www.satishmaha.com/BetterGit
comments.analysis: This comment correctly distinguishes between system-level conflict resolution by CRDTs and semantic conflicts that require human intervention, highlighting the limitations of CRDTs in version control.

comments.author: bos
comments.text:
This is sort of a revival and elaboration of some of Bram’s ideas from Codeville, an earlier effort that dates back to the early 2000s Cambrian explosion of DVCS.

Codeville also used a weave for storage and merge, a concept that originated with SCCS (and thence into Teamware and BitKeeper).

Codeville predates the introduction of CRDTs by almost a decade, and at least on the face of it the two concepts seem like a natural fit.

It was always kind of difficult to argue that weaves produced unambiguously better merge results (and more limited conflicts) than the more heuristically driven approaches of git, Mercurial, et al, because the edit histories required to produce test cases were difficult (at least for me) to reason about.

I like that Bram hasn’t let go of the problem, and is still trying out new ideas in the space.
comments.analysis: Provides historical context linking the current project to earlier version control systems and acknowledges the challenges in proving superiority of weave-based approaches over heuristics used by git and others.

comments.author: ata-sesli
comments.text:
The core separation line here seems to be Snapshot vs. Weave. Git treats history as a path between states, but Manyana treats the state as the history.

Since the weave grows with every line ever written, how do you handle "tombstone" (deleted data) bloat? In a decade-old repo with high churn, does the metadata overhead for a single file eventually make it unmanageable compared to Git’s "forgetful" snapshotting?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about metadata bloat in weave-based systems over time, which is a practical consideration for long-lived repositories.

comments.author: ulrikrasmussen
comments.text: The thing about how merges are presented seems orthogonal to how to represent history. I also hate the default in git, but that is why I just use p4merge as a merge tool and get a proper 4-pane merge tool (left, right, common base, merged result) which shows everything needed to figure out why there is a conflict and how to resolve it. I don't understand why you need to switch out the VCS to fix that issue.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical workaround for merge visualization issues in git without switching VCS, highlighting that UI improvements can be decoupled from underlying data structures.

comments.author: PunchyHamster
comments.text:
At this point if your VCS isn't a layer above git plumbing, nobody gonna waste time using it. Especially if the improvements are minor enough that it could be reasonably just a wrapper and still have 90% of the improvements.

> Two opaque blobs. You have to mentally reconstruct what actually happened.

Did you not discover what git diff does ? It's clearer than the presented improvement !

Plenty of 3 way merge tools supported by git too, sure, it's external tool but it's adding one tool rather than upending the workflow

> Conflicts are informative, not blocking. The merge always produces a result. Conflicts are surfaced for review when concurrent edits happen “too near” each other, but they never block the merge itself. And because the algorithm tracks what each side did rather than just showing the two outcomes, the conflict presentation is genuinely useful.

Git merge cache (git rerere) is good enough. Only problem is that it isn't shared but that could be possibly done within git format itself if someone really wanted to
comments.analysis: Realistically assesses the ecosystem dominance of git and suggests that incremental improvements via wrappers might be more practical than entirely new VCS.

comments.author: mikey-k
comments.text:
Interesting idea. While conflicts can be improved, I personally don't see it as a critical challenge with VCS.

What I do think is the critical challenge (particularly with Git) is scalability.

Size of repository & rate of change of repositories are starting to push limits of git, and I think this needs revisited across the server, client & wire protocols.

What exactly, I don't know. :). But I do know that in my current role (mid-size well-known tech company) is hitting these limits today.
comments.analysis: Highlights an important issue often overlooked in VCS discussions: scalability limits of git, which may be a more pressing problem than merge conflicts.

comments.author: jFriedensreich
comments.text: starts with “based on the fundamentally sound approach of using CRDTs for version control”. How on earth is crdt a sound base for a version control system? This makes no sense fundamentally, you need to reach a consistent state that is what you intended not what some crdt decided and jj shows you can do that also without blocking on merges but with first level conflicts that need to be resolved. ai and language aware merge drivers are helping so much here i really wonder if the world these “replace version control” projects were made for still exists at all.
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism about CRDTs as a foundation for VCS, reinforcing that consistent intended states require semantic conflict resolution beyond what CRDTs offer.

comments.author: BlueHotDog2
comments.text:
This is cool and i keep thinking about CRDTs as a baseline for version control, but CRDTs has some major issues, mainly the fact that most of them are strict and "magic" in the way they actually converge(like the joke: CRDTs always converge, but to what).
i didn't read if he's using some special CRDT that might solve for that, but i think that for agentic work especially this is very interesting
comments.analysis: Acknowledges the appeal of CRDTs but points out their convergence can be unpredictable, which is a valid concern for complex version control scenarios.

comments.author: toomim
comments.text:
CRDTs actually have a long history in version control.

  - The original 1977 version control system, SCCS, was a CRDT: https://braid.org/meeting-60/sccs-is-a-time-collapse

  - It called its data structure a 'weave"

  - Brahm's old project "Codeville" used a weave for version control

  - But then git blew up in popularity.

  - The project "DARCS" tried to make a robust "theory of patches," and eventually led to the development of Pijul

  - Pijul is a VCS that is a CRDT: https://pijul.org
comments.analysis: Provides accurate historical insight showing that CRDT concepts have been part of version control for decades, grounding the discussion in historical precedent.

comments.author: modeless
comments.text:
> the key insight is that changes should be flagged as conflicting when they touch each other

Not really. Changes should be flagged as conflicting when they conflict semantically, not when they touch the same lines. A rename of a variable shouldn't conflict with a refactor that touches the same lines, and a change that renames a function should conflict with a change that uses the function's old name in a new place. I don't think I would bother switching to a new VCS that didn't provide some kind of semantic understanding like this.
comments.analysis: Emphasizes the importance of semantic conflict detection over simple line-based conflict detection, a key challenge for future VCS improvements.

comments.author: mpalmer
comments.text:
This sounds more like the present of version control in the form of Jujutsu, which already supports 3-way merges and history-preserving rebases, and does so with data structures that are trivially representable in a standard git repo.

> What it is is a proof that CRDT-based version control can handle the hard UX problems and come out with better answers than the tools we’re all using today — and a coherent design for building the real thing.

No such thing is "proven". You have not proven superiority to the state of the art with 400 lines of Python.

The LLM you used to draft this blog post is making your solution out to be far more than it is at this point.
comments.analysis: Critiques the claim of novelty by pointing out existing tools like Jujutsu that already address many of the stated problems, urging caution about overhyping new approaches.

comments.author: radarsat1
comments.text:
Is it a good thing to have merges that never fail? Often a merge failure indicates a semantic conflict, not just "two changes in the same place". You want to be aware of and forced to manually deal with such cases.

I assume the proposed system addresses it somehow but I don't see it in my quick read of this.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern that automatic merges without failures might hide important semantic conflicts that require manual resolution.

comments.author: qsera
comments.text:
I think this was something that was waiting for something like LLMs to happen to be solved.

Why aren't AI companies touting "zero-shot"ing huge merge conflicts being resolved by LLMs..
comments.analysis: Speculates on the potential role of AI in future merge conflict resolution, which is a plausible direction but currently unproven at scale.

comments.author: gnarlouse
comments.text:
I think something like this needs to be born out of analysis of gradations of scales of teams using version control systems.

- What kind of problems do 1 person, 10 person, 100 person, 1k (etc) teams really run into with managing merge conflicts?

- What do teams of 1, 10, 100, 1k, etc care the most about?

- How does the modern "agent explosion" potentially affect this?

For example, my experience working in the 1-100 regime tells me that, for the most part, the kind of merge conflict being presented here is resolved by assigning subtrees of code to specific teams. For the large part, merge conflicts don't happen, because teams coordinate (in sprints) to make orthogonal changes, and long-running stale branches are discouraged.

However, if we start to mix in agents, a 100 person team could quickly jump into a 1000 person team, esp if each person is using subagents making micro commits.

It's an interesting idea definitely, but without real-world data, it kind of feels like this is just delivering a solution without a clear problem to assign it to. Like, yes merge-conflicts are a bummer, but they happen infrequently enough that it doesn't break your heart.
comments.analysis: Insightfully suggests that VCS solutions should be tailored to team size and workflows, noting that merge conflicts are often mitigated by team coordination rather than tooling alone.

comments.author: simonw
comments.text: This thing is really short. https://github.com/bramcohen/manyana/blob/main/manyana.py is 473 lines of dependency-free Python (that file only imports difflib, itertools and inspect) and of that ~240 lines are implementation and the rest are tests.
comments.analysis: Provides factual information about the implementation size and simplicity, which is useful context but does not critique the approach.

comments.author: injidup
comments.text: I'm confused about what this solves. They give the example of someone editing a function and someone deleting the same function and claim that the merge never fails and then go on to demonstrate that indeed rightly the merges still fails. There are still merge markers in the sources. What is the improvement exactly?
comments.analysis: Questions the practical improvement over existing merge conflict handling, highlighting that conflicts still appear, which challenges the claimed benefits.

comments.author: mentalgear
comments.text:
> [CRDT] This means merges don’t need to find a common ancestor or traverse the DAG. Two states go in, one state comes out, and it’s always correct.

Well, isn't that what the CRDT does in its own data structure ?

Also keep in mind that syntactic correctness doesn't mean functional correctness.
comments.analysis: Points out that CRDTs inherently avoid some traditional merge steps but cautions that syntactic correctness does not guarantee functional correctness.

comments.author: QuiCasseRien
comments.text:
I will look at it, it seems interesting. However, i hope a better ending than Pyjul (https://pijul.org/).

I'm no longer waiting for it whereas everything sound awesome : quite no more merge conflict and patches order free.

So sad it's still not production ready.
comments.analysis: Expresses cautious optimism but notes that similar projects like Pijul have struggled to reach production readiness, tempering enthusiasm.

comments.author: danpalmer
comments.text:
I'm struggling to understand the problem this solves for me. I can see in the abstract why this might be useful, but in practice I don't see the problems.

For me, jj represents a massive step forward from git in terms of usability, usefulness, and solving problems I actually have.

I think the next step forward for version control would be something that works at a lower level, such as the AST. I'd love to see an exploration of what versioning looks like when we don't have files and directories, and a piece of software is one whole tree that can be edited at any level. Things like LightTable and Dark have tried bits of this, it would be good to see a VCS demo of that sort of thing.
comments.analysis: Questions the practical necessity of the proposed approach and suggests that improvements at the AST or semantic level might be more impactful.
